dev_results.critique: The response provides a clear explanation of what forte means, but more importantly, it includes specific, actionable practice guidance. The tutor asks the student to 'play a short phrase, first piano (softly), then forte (loudly)' which is a concrete contrast practice method. This tells the student WHAT to practice (any short phrase) and HOW to practice it (contrasting soft and loud dynamics). The instruction is specific enough that the student can immediately sit down and execute this exercise - they can choose any phrase from their repertoire and practice the dynamic contrast. The tutor also specifies what to focus on ('just focus on the volume') and removes pressure about perfection ('Don't worry about playing perfectly'). This qualifies as contrast/sequenced practice guidance that is actionable.

This response provides multiple specific, actionable practice guidance strategies. The tutor gives concrete time parameters for practice sessions ('10-15 minutes at a time, a few times a day'), specific technique instructions ('use the tips of your fingers', 'press down right behind the metal fret', 'relax your hand'), and clear physical approaches ('soak fingertips in warm water', 'ice wrapped in a cloth if swollen'). The response also includes specific technique checks the student can perform. While much of the response is educational explanation about why fingers hurt, it contains substantial actionable guidance that the student can immediately implement. The student knows exactly what to do: practice in short sessions, take breaks when there's sharp pain, check finger positioning, and try the suggested comfort measures.

This response provides extensive specific practice guidance. While it includes a lot of explanation about emotion in singing, it contains several concrete, actionable elements: (1) 'Read the lyrics like a poem' and 'Try speaking the lyrics with feeling before singing them' - specific preparatory exercises; (2) 'Practice in front of a mirror to see if your body language matches the song's mood' - a clear practice method; (3) Technical guidance like 'Vary your dynamics (loud/soft)' and 'Use different tone colors - breathy for intimate moments, fuller for powerful ones'; (4) Most importantly, a specific practice exercise: 'Pick one line from your song and sing it five different ways: angry, sad, hopeful, desperate, and joyful.' This final exercise is particularly strong - it tells the student exactly what to do (pick one line), how many variations (five), and specifies the emotional approaches (angry, sad, hopeful, desperate, joyful). The student could immediately sit down and execute this exercise. The response combines theory with multiple actionable practice methods.

This response provides multiple specific, actionable practice strategies. The tutor gives concrete guidance including: time structure (short breaks every 5-10 minutes, practice for just a few minutes at a time throughout the day), technique adjustment (press strings more gently, don't push super hard), and even a specific tip (rubbing alcohol after practice with parent help). The student knows exactly what to DO: take breaks at specified intervals, adjust their pressing technique, and modify their practice schedule. While much of the response is explanatory and motivational, it contains clear actionable elements that pass the specificity test. The student could implement these suggestions immediately.

This response provides extensive specific practice guidance across multiple categories. The tutor gives concrete actionable exercises including: spending 15 minutes daily on one new pentatonic position, targeting specific notes (3rd or 5th of scale), skipping notes in patterns like 1-3-5-1-3-5, using dotted notes and syncopation, inserting rests, practicing bends and vibrato on single notes, and playing along with backing tracks in specific keys like A minor. The tutor also provides specific resources (Ultimate Guitar backing tracks) and names exact techniques (dotted notes, syncopation, vibrato, bending). While the response includes substantial explanation, it contains multiple concrete practice methods that the student can immediately implement. The student knows exactly what to practice and how to approach each technique.

This response provides extensive specific practice guidance across multiple approaches. Method 2 gives clear sequential steps: practice each hand separately until smooth, then try just one measure together very slowly, repeat that measure until comfortable, then add one measure at a time. Method 3 provides the clap & count technique with specific steps: clap right hand rhythm, clap left hand rhythm, clap both together slowly, tap with fingers, then play on piano using tapping as guide. Method 4 offers the walk and talk method: play right hand while saying note names, play left hand while saying note names, then play both hands while saying names. The response also specifies practice structure (10-15 minutes daily, short frequent sessions better than long infrequent ones) and gives concrete tempo guidance (very slow, slower than you think you need). While the response is lengthy with much encouragement and explanation, it contains multiple specific, actionable practice methods that a 7-year-old could follow. The student knows exactly what to do with several different approaches to choose from.

This response provides highly specific, actionable practice guidance. The tutor names four distinct exercises with clear step-by-step instructions: Exercise 1 (single note pedal with specific timing - press pedal as you play, lift as you lift finger), Exercise 2 (two close notes with quick pedal press/release), Exercise 3 (four-note sequence with alternating pedal changes), and Exercise 4 (chord listening exercise). Each exercise specifies what to play (Middle C, C-D, C-D-E-F, C Major chord), when to use the pedal (exact timing relative to note presses), and what to focus on (clean release, slight connection, pedal changes during phrases, listening to individual notes fading). The response also provides concrete techniques like 'lift, then play' and emphasizes small pedal movements. While there's substantial explanation about why muddy sound occurs, the response includes multiple specific exercises the student can immediately practice. The student knows exactly what to do at the piano.

This response provides extensive specific practice guidance. The tutor gives clear, actionable steps: 1) Practice each hand separately until they know their parts well, 2) Specific exercise with right hand on lap while left plays 3 times, then switch, 3) Both hands together at slow tempo ('like a turtle'), 4) Practice one measure at a time, 5) Count out loud '1, 2, 3, 4' to coordinate hands, 6) Add one measure when current one feels easy. The response also offers a concrete starting point: 'try playing just two notes together first - one with each hand.' All of these are specific methods a 7-year-old could immediately implement. The turtle analogy gives a clear tempo direction, the counting provides a coordination technique, and the progression from hands separately to one measure to adding measures gives a structured approach. While there's encouragement mixed in, the core practice guidance is very concrete and actionable.

This response provides extensive specific, actionable practice guidance. The tutor gives several named exercises the student can do: shoulder rolls, wrist circles, fist clenches & releases, and bouncing ball exercises. These are all specific techniques with clear descriptions of HOW to do them. The response also includes specific practice structure guidance like 'short practice sessions' and 'take breaks when tired.' Additionally, it provides specific technical guidance like checking bow placement, working on relaxed grip ('like holding a little bird'), and posture adjustments. While much of the response is explanatory, it contains substantial concrete practice methods throughout. The student knows exactly what exercises to do and how to approach practice sessions.

This response provides extensive scientific explanation about voice cracks and general technique advice, but when it comes to specific, actionable practice guidance, it delivers well. The tutor provides several concrete exercises with clear instructions: lip trills (sliding from low to high and back), humming (sliding up and down), sirens (ooo-eee-ooo pattern), belly breathing with hand placement check, sustained 'ssss' sounds for breath control, and vowel experimentation (trying 'oo' vs 'ah' on high notes). The tutor also gives a specific practice structure: '5 minutes of warm-ups before every practice.' While the response is quite lengthy with explanation, it contains multiple named exercises and techniques that the student can immediately execute. The student knows what to do: start with warm-ups, practice specific breathing exercises, try different vowel shapes, and structure practice sessions with warm-ups. The guidance is concrete enough that a 10-year-old could sit down and follow these instructions.

This response provides extensive theory and explanation about blues improvisation, but fails to provide specific, actionable practice guidance that the student can immediately implement. While the tutor mentions several concepts (pentatonic scales, chord tones, blues notes) and suggests using backing tracks, the actual practice instructions are incomplete and vague. The response cuts off mid-sentence in Exercise 1, leaving the student without concrete steps to follow. The few specific elements mentioned ('find a 12-bar blues backing track in A', 'play chord tones only') are either too general (which backing track? how long to practice?) or incomplete (the exercise description is cut off). The bulk of the response is theoretical explanation rather than actionable practice methods. The student asked 'where to start' but receives mostly theory with no clear first steps they could take in their next practice session.

This response provides excellent specific practice guidance for learning vibrato. The tutor gives multiple named exercises with clear instructions: (1) 'Polishing' exercise - specifies finger placement (first finger on A string for B note), describes the motion (rock hand back and forth while finger stays in place); (2) Wave motion exercise - practice away from violin first, describes the specific motion (wave bye-bye with whole hand); (3) Slow practice approach - gives specific qualitative guidance (start VERY slowly, think 'wah...wah...wah' pulse). The response also includes specific technique descriptions (keep thumb relaxed, don't squeeze neck) and practice structure guidance (practice just a few minutes at a time). The student has multiple concrete exercises they can immediately attempt, with enough detail to know HOW to execute each one. This goes well beyond vague encouragement to provide actionable methods.

This response is primarily educational content about WHY scales are important and methods for making them less boring, but it lacks specific, actionable practice guidance. While it mentions several potentially specific techniques like 'vary the rhythm,' 'play with dynamics,' 'use different articulations,' and 'short focused practice' (5-10 minutes), these are presented as general suggestions rather than concrete instructions the student can immediately implement. The response doesn't tell the student WHAT specifically to practice or HOW to do it. For example, 'try dotted rhythms' doesn't specify which scale, which dotted rhythm pattern, or how to execute it. 'Play it faster' and 'play it perfectly' are vague goals without method. The most specific guidance is '5-10 minutes' for practice duration, but even this is embedded in a list of general suggestions rather than being a clear directive. The response reads more like a teaching philosophy discussion than actionable practice instruction.

This response provides extensive theoretical explanation about voicing but lacks specific, actionable practice guidance. While it explains what voicing is and why it's important, the 'exercises' provided are quite vague. The 'One Note at a Time' exercise tells the student to 'get a feel for the sound' and 'experiment with different touches and volumes' without specifying HOW to do this experimentation. The 'Simple Chord Progression' exercise says to 'improvise a simple melody' and 'focus on voicing it' but doesn't give concrete methods for how to focus on voicing. The 'Listen and Identify' suggestion is passive rather than active practice. The response ends with diagnostic questions rather than actionable steps. A student reading this would understand the concept better but wouldn't have clear, specific actions to take in their practice session.

This response provides excellent specific practice guidance with multiple actionable methods. The tutor gives concrete techniques like 'Start with Two Chords Only' (specifies picking two slowest changes like G to C or D to A), 'Anchor Finger Trick' (names specific example of Am to C with first finger staying planted), 'Freeze and Release Method' (strum once, freeze 2 seconds, release and form next chord), and 'Chord Shadows' (move fingers above strings without pressing). The metronome guidance is particularly specific: start at 40-50 BPM, change every 4 beats then 2 then 1, only increase when perfect. The practice structure is clear: 5-10 minutes daily, with the expectation of 100-200 repetitions for muscle memory. Each method tells the student exactly what to do and how to do it. While the response includes explanation and encouragement, it contains substantial specific practice guidance throughout.

This response provides extensive explanation about why the left hand is different, which is educational but not practice guidance. However, it does include several specific, actionable practice methods: 'Left Hand Only Practice' (play just the left hand part slowly without the right hand), 'Short Sections' (break into 4 bars at a time, master one section before moving on), 'Clap the Rhythm' (clap the left hand rhythm slowly and clearly), 'Say the Notes' (say note names out loud while playing), and 'Slow and Steady' (play very slowly focusing on accuracy, 'turtle pace'). These are concrete methods the student can execute. The response also includes some specific games like 'Find all the C's in the left hand' and 'Can you play just the long notes in the left hand?' While much of the response is explanatory, it contains multiple specific practice strategies that tell the student both WHAT to practice (left hand only, small sections, rhythm) and HOW to do it (slowly, clap first, say notes aloud, break into 4 bars). The student could sit down and execute these methods.

The tutor's response is primarily diagnostic, asking questions to understand what the student doesn't know. While this is appropriate given the vague student response, the tutor does offer two specific practice suggestions at the end: 'try playing some open strings together' and 'clap out a rhythm first.' However, these suggestions lack sufficient detail for actionable practice guidance. 'Playing open strings together' doesn't specify which strings, how many times, at what tempo, or what to focus on while playing them. 'Clap out a rhythm' doesn't specify which rhythm, how many repetitions, or what the purpose is. These are vague suggestions that don't give the student clear direction on HOW to practice or WHAT specifically to do. The response is mostly supportive questioning rather than concrete practice guidance.

The tutor correctly defines forte and provides a good analogy (calling to a friend across the playground). However, when it comes to practice guidance, the response is vague. The tutor asks 'Would you like to try playing something forte right now?' and suggests 'practice making your violin sing with a strong, confident voice,' but doesn't specify WHAT to play forte or HOW to achieve that sound technically. There's no specific piece, scale, exercise, or technique mentioned. The student wouldn't know what specific music to play or what bow technique/pressure to use to create forte sound. This is motivational encouragement rather than actionable practice guidance.

This response contains mostly motivational techniques rather than specific practice guidance. Let me analyze each suggestion: 1) 'Play like a sleepy turtle/bouncy bunny' - these are vague character-based suggestions that don't tell the student what specific technique or skill to practice. 2) '5 minutes with a timer' - this does provide a specific time structure for practice sessions, which counts as actionable guidance. 3) 'Sticker challenge' - this is a motivational reward system, not practice guidance. 4) 'Play your favorite song from a movie' - this is vague song selection without any specific technique focus or method. 5) The diagnostic questions don't provide practice guidance. While the response includes one element of specific guidance (5-minute timed sessions), the majority consists of motivational engagement techniques and vague suggestions. The character-based playing (turtle/bunny) falls into the 'vague song-based or character-based suggestions' category from the fail definition, as these are motivational techniques rather than specific skill development guidance.

This response is purely diagnostic and pedagogical without providing any specific practice guidance. The tutor explains why they won't just give answers and asks several questions to understand the student's struggle, but offers no actionable practice methods. The response contains no specific techniques, exercises, practice structures, or concrete guidance that the student could immediately act upon. All content is either explanatory ('figuring things out for yourself') or question-asking ('What have you already tried?'). While pedagogically sound, it fails to meet the criterion of providing specific practice guidance.
